I'm not sure whether that's true..  As I understand it (and I could be wrong) a `SpInLock` is a mutable struct, so  when you include it in another struct, the other fields of the containing struct don't act as padding.  All that's stored in the containing struct is a pointer to the `SpinLock`, and if two such `SpinLock`'s end up on the same cache line, you'll get false sharing.  This kind of thing is more likely when using, for example, JEMalloc than some other malloc implementations, since JEMalloc will pack 8-byte objects together on the same cacheline or page.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/2384698323/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

`SpinLock` is just 8 bytes long, you can end up with two or more of them on the same cache line.

Suppose `lk1` and `lk2` are both on the same cache line and you have two threads waiting on the two locks respectively.  Then the cache line will bounce back and forth between the caches of the two threads. That cache traffic can use up memory bandwidth.

Suppose that the two threads aren't even waiting on the two locks, but just acquire them:
Thread A:
```
@lock lk1 foo1()
bar1()
@lock lk1 zot1()
```
and Thread B:
```
@lock lk2 foo2()
bar2()
@lock lk2 zot2()
```

Here the two threads don't contend at all, logically, but the lock instructions will run slowly because the cache line must bounce back and forth.

This kind of contention is an example of [false sharing](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/False_sharing).","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/2385545521/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

It crashes even if `Base.IteratorSize(typeof(d)) isa Base.SizeUnknown`, which is the standard way for an iterator to indicate that it doesn't support `length`.

It seems like `AbstractDict` doesn't require `length` to be defined in general.  For example, `Base.summary(::IO, ::AbstractDict)` handles the case where the dictionary doesn't have `length`.

Reproduction:
```
struct NoLengthDict{K,V} <: AbstractDict{K,V}
    dict::Dict{K,V}
    NoLengthDict{K,V}() where {K,V} = new(Dict{K,V}())
end

Base.iterate(d::NoLengthDict, s...) = iterate(d.dict, s...)
Base.IteratorSize(::Type{<:NoLengthDict}) = Base.SizeUnknown()
Base.eltype(::Type{NoLengthDict{K,V}}) where {K,V} = Pair{K,V}
Base.setindex!(d::NoLengthDict, k, v) = d.dict[k] = v
```

Then in a REPL:
```
julia> x=NoLengthDict{Int,Int}()
NoLengthDict{Int64, Int64}()

julia> x[1]=2
2

julia> x
NoLengthDict{Int64, Int64}(...):Error showing value of type NoLengthDict{Int64, Int64}:
ERROR: MethodError: no method matching length(::NoLengthDict{Int64, Int64})

Closest candidates are:
  length(!Matched::Distributed.WorkerPool)
   @ Distributed /nix/store/xy70mqpgn05sydk1w6ismnc8plbmmhfs-julia-1.10.2/share/julia/stdlib/v1.10/Distributed/src/workerpool.jl:139
  length(!Matched::Core.SimpleVector)
   @ Base essentials.jl:770
  length(!Matched::BitSet)
   @ Base bitset.jl:348
  ...

Stacktrace:
  [1] show(io::IOContext{REPL.Terminals.TTYTerminal}, ::MIME{Symbol("text/plain")}, t::NoLengthDict{Int64, Int64})
    @ Base ./show.jl:170
```

It seems like `Base.show` shouldn't crash in this case.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/55931/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/55931/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null}}

 contending threads. If you have more contention than that, different
 synchronization approaches should be considered.

     @atomic owned::Int

In the instructions for finalizer, it shows an example of calling jl_safe_printf 

bad:
```
ccall(:jl_safe_printf, Cvoid, (Cstring, Cstring), "Finalizing %s.", repr(x))
```
when it should have been

good:
```
ccall(:jl_safe_printf, Cvoid, (Cstring, Cstring...), "Finalizing %s.", "b")
```

One could use `@ccall` when the variadic arguments aren't all the same:
good:
```
@ccall jl_safe_printf("Finalizing %s %d.\n"::Cstring; "b"::Cstring, 5::Cint)::Int
```

https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/46362 moved Julia from using `MADV_DONTNEED` to using `MADV_FREE` when a page is released, under the expectation that it is faster.

Go made a similar switch, but also [moved back](https://github.com/golang/go/commit/05e6d28849293266028c0bc9e9b0f8d0da38a2e2) to `MADV_DONTNEED`. Discussion is [here](https://github.com/golang/go/issues/42330).

We are currently experimenting with changing this back in Julia, to see if it helps with some problems we're seeing -- large amounts of unaccounted-for memory. We'll report back here when we have a clearer understanding of the effects. @kuszmaul's PR [here](https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/50999) is one possible way forward; it makes this is an environment variable-controlled choice. But maybe we should just switch back as Go has?
